course_description:
Engineering principles, associated calculations relevant to fundamental food processing operations, including heat transfer & heat exchangers, thermodynamics, gases & vapours, steam, psychrometry, mass & energy balances, fluids & fluid flow, basic viscosity and rheology, refrigeration, reaction kinetics & instrumentation & control.

This course involves the fundamental learnings on physical principles, associated calculations & basic equipment relevant to food processing operations, including heat transfer & heat exchangers, thermodynamics, gases & vapours, steam, psychrometry, mass & energy balances, fluids & fluid flow, basic rheology, refrigeration,ﾠinstrumentation & control and reaction kinetics in food processing.

incompatible: You can't enrol in this course if you've already completed the following:

However, the mid-semester and final examinations will be different for each course. Additionally, the poster assessment differs between the two. In this course, students are required to submit one poster and one report , both of which must include mandatory references . Furthermore, the assessment must be completed in pairs (groups of two students) .

course_aims: The aim of the subject is to provide students with an overview of food engineering principles and processes involved in the manufacture of food products and to provide practice in the use of these principles to solve real industrial problems.

learning_outcomes.description: Apply advanced principles of thermodynamics, units and dimensions, and fluid mechanics in practical situations in food engineering.
Utilize in-depth knowledge of heat and mass transfer to evaluate and enhance techniques used in food processing and preservation.
Analyze the roles of phase and state transitions, food freezing, and interfacial phenomena in influencing the structure and properties of food materials.
Evaluate separation processes, resource sustainability, and control systems, proposing improvements to enhance efficiency and sustainability in food processing.

Demonstrate advanced problem-solving skills and critical thinking by analyzing complex scenarios, performing precise calculations, and developing practical and innovative solutions in food engineering.

assessment_details.title: Presentation (Poster style) and Report
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment covers Modules 1 to 12 and evaluates students on teamwork (group of 2) and their practical understanding of key course concepts. Assessment Structure: 1. Poster Presentation: Students must create a poster and record an oral presentation following this structure: Introduction, Experimental Setup, Main Results, Discussion, Conclusion, and References . 2. Written Report: In addition to the poster presentation, students must submit a written report providing a detailed narrative of their work. The report should be a maximum of 4 A4 pages . Main Topics & Group Work: The assessment will focus on home-based experiments , introduced during Week 6 tutorials . Experiments will be conducted in groups , and the final mark will be shared equally among group members . Marking criteria: Clarity of speech (10%): This refers to how engaging the speech is - every individual should say something. Content (25%): This refers to the understanding of the home-made experiment and the theory behind it. Style and layout (25%): This refers to the quality of pictures, images, layout, and flow. Critical point of view (30%): explanation of why certain events and provide reasons and solutions. Reference (10%): Use of proper references. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submission Instructions:
You can submit multiple times; the final uploaded version will be marked. To avoid duplications, one student from the group does the submission. Please follow the guide shared in blackboard to submit the file (power point file with video recorded included). You can upload your video file to Echo along the way.
Student Guide:
Submit a Video with EchoVideo There are several steps in the process, so allow yourself plenty of time to ensure you complete the process before the submission deadline.
Technical issue?
Contact the UQ Library AskUs Team
Groups will be formed within the first five weeks of the course. Details on the submission process will be provided on Blackboard and explained during the Week 6 tutorial .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (24 Feb - 01 Jun)
learning_activities.topic: Lectures Explanation of the following subjects: material properties, thermodynamics, mass and energy balance, fluid mechanics, heat transfer, mass transfer, psychrometry, sustainable processes, refrigeration, dehydration, and evaporation systems, sustainability, and instrument control. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
